ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers laksa as a category; no specific dry-style laksa with seafood entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup dish — laksa type (closest parent; dry variant not separately coded in FoodEx2 matrix)

main_ingredients: homemade rempah (spice paste: lemongrass, galangal, dried shrimp, candlenut, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of dry laksa with seafood is approximately 380 g, comprising noodles, seafood, rempah sauce, coconut cream, cherry tomatoes, arugula, and chilli oil.

nutrient_source.title: Laksa (coconut-based noodle dish) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimation for dry-style laksa with seafood
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for standard Singapore laksa (coconut milk noodle soup). Dry laksa with seafood differs in that the broth is reduced and tossed rather than served as soup, resulting in higher fat and energy density per 100 g due to concentrated coconut cream and chilli oil. Values adapted from HPB laksa entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for coconut cream (USDA #170172), rice noodles (USDA #20113), and mixed seafood. Per-100g values represent the composite dish as plated.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles and coconut milk mixed dish — analogous GI estimate based on rice vermicelli GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry laksa with seafood. GI estimated at 53 based on the primary carbohydrate carrier being thick rice vermicelli (GI ~53 per University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles, boiled). Coconut cream fat content moderates glycemic response. Seafood and arugula contribute negligible carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (53 × 56.2) / 100 ≈ 29.8, rounded to 29.9. Serving carbohydrate = 14.8 g/100g × 380 g = 56.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1596 mg per serving) from rempah paste, dried shrimp, and chilli oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit dry laksa with seafood to occasional consumption and request reduced chilli oil and rempah to lower sodium intake toward the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~30 per serving) driven by rice noodle carbohydrate content, partially offset by coconut fat and seafood protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a half-portion (approximately 190 g) to limit glycemic load, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the moderate-to-high carbohydrate load per full serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut cream (~6.1 g/100 g; ~23 g per serving) which may raise LDL cholesterol with frequent consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of dry laksa with seafood to once weekly or less, as the high saturated fat from coconut cream exceeds one-third of the recommended daily saturated fat limit in a single serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Laksa with Seafood is a contemporary Singaporean noodle dish that reimagines the classic coconut-based laksa soup in a dry, sauce-tossed format. Unlike traditional laksa which is served in a rich coconut milk broth, the dry version features thick rice vermicelli noodles coated in a concentrated rempah (spice paste) made from lemongrass, galangal, dried shrimp, candlenut, and chilli, combined with reduced coconut cream and chilli oil. The dish is topped with mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, or shellfish — along with cherry tomatoes and arugula (rocket leaves), which add freshness and a peppery contrast to the rich, spiced sauce. The flavour profile is intensely aromatic, mildly spicy, creamy from the coconut, and umami-rich from the dried shrimp in the rempah. Texturally, the noodles are soft and slippery, coated in a glossy, fragrant sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from seafood (~8.5 g/100 g), significant saturated fat from coconut cream (~6.1 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from rice noodles (~14.8 g/100 g). Sodium is high (~420 mg/100 g) due to the rempah and chilli oil, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), but the glycemic load per full serving is approximately 30, which is clinically significant for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal. It is a fusion-influenced restaurant dish found in modern Singaporean cafes and casual dining establishments.

Calories in dry laksa with seafood
What seafood is in dry laksa?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for dry laksa with seafood as a distinct dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore laksa (coconut noodle soup) data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data. The dry preparation concentrates fat and energy relative to the soup version. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-documented and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Cuisine corrected from 'Italian' (input error) to 'Singaporean' — the dish is a Singaporean noodle dish with Peranakan/Malay culinary roots. The input cuisine tags ('Italian', 'Pizza', 'Pasta') are incorrect and have been discarded; they appear to be a data entry error from the field survey seed record. 2. The restaurant 'Mingle - Funan' serves this as a modern fusion interpretation of laksa, which explains the non-traditional garnishes (arugula, cherry tomatoes). 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or USDA source data for this dish category. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; this is confirmed as negligible/trace in coconut-cream-based dishes per USDA ingredient data — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for coconut cream and rice noodle components. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA ingredient-level data for coconut cream and seafood; these are not directly reported in HPB Foodo for this dish. 6. GI set to 53 (low) based on rice vermicelli analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic_load_per_serving = 29.9 based on 380 g serving size and 14.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g. 7. Price data (SGD 36.00) preserved from input as restaurant-sourced contextual information only; not used in nutritional calculations.
